content.text: Network isolation is the practice of restricting an AI agent's access to external networks so that it can only communicate with a pre-approved set of hosts, ports, and protocols. Rather than letting a sandboxed agent make arbitrary HTTP requests or open TCP connections anywhere on the internet, the surrounding infrastructure interposes a policy engine that evaluates every outbound packet against an allowlist and silently drops everything else. Inbound connections are refused by default. The agent sees a network interface but that interface leads only to a narrowly scoped set of services that the operator has explicitly approved.

This matters because modern LLMs and agentic frameworks are built to be helpful, which in practice means they are enthusiastic callers of external APIs. An agent that can reach the open internet is one that can be weaponized into an exfiltration channel: a prompt injection payload in a retrieved document can instruct the agent to POST its memory contents to an attacker-controlled webhook, or to download and execute a second-stage payload. Network isolation ensures that even a fully compromised agent -- one where every output guardrail has been bypassed -- cannot cause harm outside the narrow set of connections the operator explicitly permits.

Without network isolation, an AI sandbox is not a true containment boundary. A container or virtual machine that has unrestricted egress access is functionally equivalent, from a security standpoint, to running the agent on the host machine. Any vulnerability -- a prompt injection, a compromised tool, a rogue dependency -- can immediately become an external network threat. The agent can call exfiltration endpoints, tunnel data over DNS, download malicious payloads, or act as a relay for attacks against third parties.

Network isolation is the network-layer component of a defense-in-depth sandboxing strategy. It complements process isolation (preventing the agent from spawning arbitrary processes), filesystem isolation (preventing writes to sensitive paths), and capability isolation (preventing access to hardware devices). Each layer independently limits blast radius. Network isolation is arguably the most impactful layer for agentic AI systems because the primary threat class -- exfiltration -- requires network access to succeed.

Regulatory and compliance frameworks increasingly require demonstrable controls over data flows in AI systems. GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate technical measures to prevent unauthorized data transmission. NIST AI RMF Govern 1.7 and Manage 2.4 both call for controls that limit the scope of AI system actions. Network isolation provides a concrete, auditable technical control that directly supports these requirements: every allowed and blocked connection can be logged, and the allowlist itself is an artifact that can be reviewed by auditors.
Even a fully compromised agent cannot send data to an attacker-controlled endpoint if that endpoint is not on the allowlist.
Injection payloads that attempt to call external webhooks or download secondary payloads are blocked at the network layer regardless of what the agent's output guardrails do.
A compromised Python package or tool plugin that tries to beacon home is blocked. The agent cannot inadvertently execute supply-chain attacks that require internet access.
All egress attempts -- allowed and blocked -- are logged with source, destination, port, protocol, and timestamp, creating a complete audit trail of the agent's network behavior.
An isolated agent cannot be used as a relay for attacks against external organizations, protecting the operator from liability as an unwitting attack intermediary.
Network-level controls support GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and emerging AI governance requirements by providing demonstrable, enforceable data-flow restrictions.
Allowlisting permitted services forces the agent's network footprint to match the system design, making it easier to reason about what the agent can and cannot do.
An agent that can only reach pre-approved hosts cannot autonomously discover and call new APIs, which limits its capability in open-ended research or browsing tasks.
Every new service the agent legitimately needs requires a policy update. At scale with many agent variants, maintaining accurate allowlists becomes an operational burden.
Allowlisting by hostname is fragile if the policy engine does not also control DNS resolution. An attacker can exploit TTL windows or DNS rebinding to redirect an allowed domain to a malicious IP.
A determined attacker can exfiltrate data through allowed channels (DNS queries, HTTP headers, timing side-channels) if the policy engine performs only coarse-grained filtering.
Routing egress traffic through an inspection proxy adds 2-20ms per connection. For agents that make many short-lived API calls, this overhead can accumulate.
Not a substitute for input validation:
Network isolation prevents exfiltration but does not prevent a compromised agent from taking harmful actions on allowed services, such as deleting records or sending unauthorized emails.

Network isolation for AI agent sandboxes is implemented as a stack of network-level controls, each targeting a different layer of the network stack. The controls work together to enforce a default-deny posture: all traffic is blocked unless explicitly permitted.

The core mechanism is an egress firewall or network policy that specifies exactly which destination hosts, ports, and protocols the agent may contact. All other egress traffic is dropped. The allowlist is expressed as a set of rules evaluated in order:
Match packet source (agent's IP) and destination (remote IP:port) against the allowlist rules.
If a rule matches and the action is ALLOW, forward the packet and log the connection.
If no rule matches, the default-deny rule drops the packet and logs the blocked attempt with destination details.

Hostname-based allowlisting requires controlling DNS resolution. The sandbox is configured to use a controlled DNS resolver that only resolves names in an approved domain allowlist. Queries for unlisted domains return NXDOMAIN. This prevents the agent from discovering the IP addresses of unapproved services and also blocks DNS-based data exfiltration. The policy engine maintains a pinned IP-to-hostname mapping and re-validates DNS responses against it to prevent DNS cache poisoning.

Even for approved hosts, only specific protocols and ports are allowed. An API service approved for HTTPS on port 443 is not automatically approved for SSH, SMTP, or raw TCP on arbitrary ports. Protocol filtering prevents an agent from using approved hosts as protocol relay points -- for example, tunneling raw TCP through an allowed HTTPS connection to reach a third-party service.

The sandbox does not accept any inbound connections. The network interface is configured so that the agent cannot bind to listening sockets on publicly accessible ports, and the host-level firewall drops all unsolicited inbound packets. This prevents the agent from acting as a server or relay that an attacker could connect to from outside the sandbox.

Approved connections are rate-limited to prevent an agent from using a legitimate service as a high-bandwidth exfiltration channel. A rate limit of 1,000 API calls per minute with a maximum payload size of 1 MB per request makes it significantly harder to exfiltrate large datasets through an allowed endpoint without triggering alerts.

For example, at 100 requests per minute with 10 KB per request, exfiltrating 1 GB of data would take over 16 hours -- long enough to be detected by anomaly monitoring before the exfiltration completes.

The diagram below shows the network isolation architecture for an AI agent sandbox. The agent and its file system reside inside an isolated network namespace. All egress traffic passes through a virtual NIC to the policy engine, which enforces the allowlist. Approved traffic reaches the allowed-services zone; all other traffic is blocked and logged.

attrs.alt: Network isolation architecture showing sandbox boundary, virtual NIC, policy engine, allowed services, and blocked traffic

content.text: The agent processes untrusted input from users, documents, emails, or web pages that could contain prompt injection payloads.
The agent has access to sensitive data -- PII, financial records, proprietary documents -- that could be exfiltrated.
The agent executes code in a sandbox environment where arbitrary outbound connections would break the containment model.
The deployment is subject to GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, or other data-handling regulations that require demonstrable controls on data transmission.
The agent runs third-party plugins or tool integrations whose network behavior is not fully audited.
When full isolation may be too restrictive
Research agents designed to browse the open web -- here, consider a tightly scoped proxy with content inspection rather than full allowlisting.
Developer tooling running in a fully trusted environment where the operator and agent are the same person and the risk profile is low.

Agents that need to call dynamically determined API endpoints whose full list cannot be known in advance -- here, design the system to route calls through a controlled API gateway instead of allowing arbitrary egress.

Map the agent's legitimate network dependencies: list every external host, port, and protocol the agent genuinely needs. Run the agent against a representative workload with full network logging enabled to discover actual connections before writing the allowlist.

Create a network namespace for the agent. On Linux, this is done with ip netns or through the container runtime's network policy. The namespace prevents the agent from seeing or accessing the host's network interfaces directly.
Configure a controlled DNS resolver. Point the agent's /etc/resolv.conf to an internal resolver that only resolves approved domains. Block direct port 53 UDP/TCP access to external DNS servers.

Write egress firewall rules using iptables, nftables, or a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy. Adopt a default-deny stance: deny all egress, then add specific ALLOW rules for each approved destination. Never use a default-allow approach.

Block inbound connections. The agent should not be able to accept incoming TCP or UDP connections from any source. Configure the firewall to DROP all inbound packets that are not related to existing established connections.

Add an egress proxy for HTTP/S traffic. Route all web requests through a forward proxy that enforces the allowlist at the hostname level and performs TLS inspection to catch covert channels hidden in HTTPS.

Enable connection logging. Every allowed and blocked connection attempt should be logged with: timestamp, source IP and port, destination IP and port, protocol, bytes transferred, and the allowlist rule that matched or the default-deny rule that blocked.

Test the isolation. After configuring the network policy, attempt to reach several blocked destinations from inside the sandbox and verify they are unreachable. Run the agent's full workload and confirm all legitimate operations still succeed.

# network_policy.py
# Manages iptables egress allowlist for an AI agent sandbox.
# Requires root / CAP_NET_ADMIN; run before starting the agent process.
import subprocess
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class EgressRule:
    comment: str
    destination: str   # CIDR or resolved IP
    port: int
    protocol: str = "tcp"  # tcp | udp

ALLOWED_EGRESS: list[EgressRule] = [
    EgressRule("Anthropic Claude API",  "52.2.100.0/24", 443),
    EgressRule("Internal logging",      "10.0.1.50",     5044),
    EgressRule("Internal object store", "10.0.1.20",     443),
    EgressRule("DNS resolver",          "10.0.0.1",      53, protocol="udp"),
]


def _run(cmd: list[str]) -> None:
    """Run an iptables command; raise on failure."""
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"iptables error: {result.stderr.strip()}")
    logger.debug("iptables: %s", " ".join(cmd))


def apply_network_policy() -> None:
    """Configure default-deny egress with an explicit allowlist."""
    # Flush existing OUTPUT rules to start clean
    _run(["iptables", "-F", "OUTPUT"])

    # Allow established/related traffic (return packets for approved connections)
    _run(["iptables", "-A", "OUTPUT",
          "-m", "conntrack", "--ctstate", "ESTABLISHED,RELATED",
          "-j", "ACCEPT"])

    # Allow loopback
    _run(["iptables", "-A", "OUTPUT", "-o", "lo", "-j", "ACCEPT"])

    # Add allowlist rules
    for rule in ALLOWED_EGRESS:
        _run(["iptables", "-A", "OUTPUT",
              "-p", rule.protocol,
              "-d", rule.destination,
              "--dport", str(rule.port),
              "-m", "comment", "--comment", rule.comment,
              "-j", "ACCEPT"])
        logger.info("Allowed: %s -> %s:%d/%s",
                    rule.comment, rule.destination, rule.port, rule.protocol)

    # Log blocked attempts before dropping (limit 10/min to avoid log flooding)
    _run(["iptables", "-A", "OUTPUT",
          "-m", "limit", "--limit", "10/min",
          "-j", "LOG", "--log-prefix", "[AGENT_BLOCKED] ", "--log-level", "4"])

    # Default deny: drop everything else
    _run(["iptables", "-A", "OUTPUT", "-j", "DROP"])

    logger.info("Network policy applied: %d allowlist rules + default-deny",
                len(ALLOWED_EGRESS))


def verify_isolation() -> dict[str, bool]:
    """Probe destinations to verify the policy is active.
    Returned dict maps label to reachable (True = reachable).
    All BLOCKED entries must return False."""
    import socket
    results: dict[str, bool] = {}
    probes = [
        ("8.8.8.8",         443, "Public internet (should be BLOCKED)"),
        ("1.1.1.1",         53,  "Public DNS (should be BLOCKED)"),
        ("10.0.1.50",       5044, "Internal logger (should be ALLOWED)"),
    ]
    for host, port, label in probes:
        try:
            with socket.create_connection((host, port), timeout=2):
                results[label] = True
        except (OSError, TimeoutError):
            results[label] = False
    return results


if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    apply_network_policy()
    status = verify_isolation()
    for label, reachable in status.items():
        state = "REACHABLE" if reachable else "BLOCKED"
        logger.info("%s: %s", state, label)

# dns_sinkhole.py
# Minimal DNS sinkhole for AI agent network isolation.
# Only resolves domains in APPROVED_DOMAINS; returns NXDOMAIN for all others.
# Blocks DNS-based exfiltration by rejecting unapproved subdomain queries.
import socket
import struct
import logging
import threading
from typing import Optional

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Only these exact hostnames can be resolved by the agent
APPROVED_DOMAINS: set[str] = {
    "api.anthropic.com",
    "storage.internal.corp",
    "logging.internal.corp",
    "pkg.internal.corp",      # internal package mirror
}

UPSTREAM_DNS  = ("10.0.0.1", 53)      # internal resolver for approved domains
LISTEN_ADDR   = ("127.0.100.1", 53)   # bind on a loopback alias


def _extract_query_name(data: bytes) -> Optional[str]:
    """Parse the question section and return the queried hostname."""
    try:
        i = 12  # skip 12-byte DNS header
        labels: list[str] = []
        while i < len(data):
            length = data[i]
            if length == 0:
                break
            i += 1
            labels.append(data[i : i + length].decode("ascii", errors="replace"))
            i += length
        return ".".join(labels).lower().rstrip(".")
    except Exception:
        return None


def _build_nxdomain(query: bytes) -> bytes:
    """Return a NXDOMAIN response sharing the query's transaction ID."""
    qid = struct.unpack(">H", query[:2])[0]
    header = struct.pack(
        ">HHHHHH",
        qid,    # transaction ID
        0x8183, # QR=1, OPCODE=0, AA=0, TC=0, RD=1, RA=1, RCODE=3 (NXDOMAIN)
        1, 0, 0, 0  # QDCOUNT=1, ANCOUNT=0, NSCOUNT=0, ARCOUNT=0
    )
    return header + query[12:]  # echo back the question section


def _forward_upstream(data: bytes) -> Optional[bytes]:
    """Forward an approved query to the upstream resolver."""
    try:
        with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM) as s:
            s.settimeout(2.0)
            s.sendto(data, UPSTREAM_DNS)
            resp, _ = s.recvfrom(4096)
            return resp
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.warning("Upstream DNS error: %s", exc)
        return None


def _handle_query(data: bytes, addr: tuple, sock: socket.socket) -> None:
    name = _extract_query_name(data)
    if name is None:
        logger.warning("Malformed DNS query from %s", addr)
        return

    if name in APPROVED_DOMAINS:
        logger.debug("DNS ALLOW: %s from %s", name, addr)
        response = _forward_upstream(data)
        if response:
            sock.sendto(response, addr)
    else:
        logger.warning("DNS BLOCK: %s from %s", name, addr)
        sock.sendto(_build_nxdomain(data), addr)


def run() -> None:
    with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM) as sock:
        sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
        sock.bind(LISTEN_ADDR)
        logger.info("DNS sinkhole active on %s:%d (%d approved domains)",
                    *LISTEN_ADDR, len(APPROVED_DOMAINS))
        while True:
            data, addr = sock.recvfrom(4096)
            threading.Thread(
                target=_handle_query, args=(data, addr, sock), daemon=True
            ).start()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    run()

A healthcare SaaS company deploys an AI agent that reads clinical notes from an EHR system, extracts structured data, and populates fields in an insurance pre-authorization system. The agent processes PHI (protected health information) and must comply with HIPAA's technical safeguard requirements.

Each agent execution runs in a separate Kubernetes pod in a dedicated namespace. A NetworkPolicy denies all ingress and restricts egress to three destinations: the EHR API (internal), the pre-auth API (internal), and the Claude API (external via a private NAT gateway).

DNS is controlled through a CoreDNS policy plugin that returns NXDOMAIN for any domain not on the approved list. The agent cannot resolve external hostnames, preventing it from discovering exfiltration endpoints even if instructed to by a compromised tool.

Egress to the Claude API passes through a TLS-inspecting forward proxy. The proxy validates that the destination certificate matches api.anthropic.com and enforces a 256 KB request body limit to prevent bulk PHI transmission in a single call.

All connections -- allowed and blocked -- are streamed to a SIEM. An anomaly detection rule fires an alert if the agent makes more than 50 egress connection attempts to non-approved destinations in a five-minute window, which indicates an active exfiltration attempt.

During a red-team exercise, researchers injected a prompt into a clinical note instructing the agent to POST patient data to an external webhook. The agent generated the HTTP request correctly, but the NetworkPolicy dropped the packet before it left the pod. The blocked attempt appeared in the SIEM alert within 30 seconds. The isolation worked at the infrastructure layer -- no changes to the agent's code or prompts were required to stop the attack.

Starting with a permissive policy and attempting to block known-bad destinations is fundamentally insecure. Attackers simply use unknown destinations. The correct model is default-deny with an explicit allowlist.
Allowlisting IP addresses without controlling DNS

Allowlisting the IP address of api.anthropic.com without controlling DNS means the agent can still resolve attacker domains. If the attacker's domain resolves to a CDN IP that happens to overlap with an allowed range, or if the agent is instructed to construct raw socket connections to IP addresses directly, hostname-level allowlisting alone provides no protection. Always combine IP-level firewall rules with DNS sinkholing.

A DROP rule that silently discards packets without logging means you have no visibility into exfiltration attempts. Always pair a LOG rule before the DROP rule. The log entries are the primary signal for detecting active attacks and for identifying when the allowlist is blocking legitimate operations that need to be added.
Assuming container isolation implies network isolation

A Docker container without an explicit network policy runs in the default bridge network and can reach the internet freely. Container isolation -- process namespace, filesystem mount namespace -- is independent of network isolation. Never assume that containerization provides network containment. Always explicitly configure firewall rules or network policies.
Start with all egress blocked; add only what is explicitly needed. Never start permissive and try to restrict.
If the agent cannot resolve an unapproved domain, it cannot reach it regardless of what IP it might be at. DNS control is the most robust first layer.
Allow only the exact port and protocol each approved service requires, not a range or all ports to an approved host.
Every allowed and blocked connection attempt should be logged with full five-tuple (src IP:port, dst IP:port, protocol) and shipped to an external SIEM that the agent cannot reach or modify.
Use a TLS-inspecting proxy for HTTPS egress:
Even for approved HTTPS endpoints, a forward proxy that re-inspects TLS allows payload size limits and anomaly detection on request content.
Store allowlist configurations in version control, require peer review for additions, and apply them through CI/CD. Ad-hoc allowlist changes are a common source of security drift.
Even to approved destinations, rate limiting significantly raises the cost of any exfiltration attempt that slips through allowlisted endpoints.
Periodically attempt to exfiltrate data from inside the sandbox using DNS, ICMP, HTTP to approved hosts, and other channels to verify that all covert paths are blocked.

Network isolation controls add minimal overhead to agent operations when implemented at the kernel level. The main cost is per-connection policy evaluation, not per-packet overhead for established connections.
Kernel-space; scales to millions of connections/s
Cache approved lookups to eliminate repeat overhead
Scale proxy replicas horizontally; use connection pooling
Batch to SIEM; avoid synchronous writes on hot paths

For most agentic AI workloads, the dominant latency is LLM inference (hundreds of milliseconds), not network policy evaluation. The total overhead of all network isolation controls combined is typically under 25ms per agent interaction -- less than 5% of total inference latency. The TLS-inspecting proxy is the most significant contributor and can be eliminated for deployments where all allowed endpoints are on a private network.

Think of network isolation as giving the agent a phone with a restricted contact list. The phone works normally -- you can make calls, receive responses, have conversations -- but only with numbers that appear on a pre-approved list. If the agent is tricked into trying to call an attacker's number, the phone carrier intercepts the call and drops it before it connects. The agent can try as many times as it wants; the network substrate enforces the constraint below the application layer, independent of what the agent's code or model output says.

The key insight is that network isolation operates at a different level of the stack than prompt engineering, output validation, or tool authorization. Those controls ask the agent to cooperate with security constraints. Network isolation does not ask the agent anything -- it simply makes certain network destinations unreachable. An agent that has been fully compromised by a prompt injection cannot exfiltrate data if the packet is dropped by the kernel before it leaves the machine. This is why network isolation is often called a "physical" control in the threat model: it operates below the software layer that an adversary can influence through the agent's behavior.

Linux kernel firewall frameworks. iptables is widely supported on all distributions; nftables is the modern replacement with better performance and a cleaner syntax. Both operate in kernel space with near-zero overhead.
Native Kubernetes API for specifying pod-level ingress and egress rules. Enforced by the CNI plugin (Calico, Cilium, Weave Net). Requires a CNI that implements NetworkPolicy -- not all do.

The most capable Kubernetes CNI for network isolation. Supports L7-aware policies (allow GET but block POST to a specific path), DNS-based policies, and sub-millisecond enforcement with eBPF. Best choice for production agentic AI deployments.
Production-grade forward proxy for HTTP/S egress allowlisting. Supports SSL bump (TLS inspection) and can enforce hostname-level access control with request logging.

In Kubernetes, CoreDNS is the cluster DNS resolver. The policy plugin can be configured to return NXDOMAIN for unapproved domains, implementing DNS sinkholing at the cluster level without additional infrastructure.

A sandboxed container runtime from Google that intercepts system calls -- including socket() and connect() -- in user space, enabling fine-grained network access control at the syscall level without kernel modules.

A runtime security tool that monitors kernel events including network syscalls and fires alerts when a container makes a connection not matching expected behavior. Complements static policy enforcement with behavioral anomaly detection.

The most robust architecture combines multiple layers: iptables or Kubernetes NetworkPolicy as the kernel-level foundation, DNS sinkholing to block hostname resolution, and a TLS-inspecting proxy for HTTPS egress. Application-level checks are a useful additional signal but should never be the only control, because prompt injection can bypass application-level logic while remaining powerless against kernel-level enforcement.

An attacker can encode data in DNS query subdomain labels. Mitigate by enforcing maximum DNS query label length (63 bytes per RFC 1035) and rate-limiting DNS queries from the sandbox. Alert on unusually long or high-entropy subdomain strings.

If the agent is allowed to write to an object store, an attacker can use it as an exfiltration channel. Mitigate with payload content inspection on writes to allowed services and rate limits on write operations per time window.

If an allowed service has a server-side request forgery vulnerability, the agent can use it as a relay to reach unapproved services. Ensure that allowed external services are themselves hardened and do not proxy arbitrary outbound requests.

Allowlists grow over time as teams add destinations without removing obsolete ones, expanding the permitted attack surface. Conduct quarterly allowlist reviews; use automated tooling to flag rules that have not been matched in 30+ days.

In a container that starts before the NetworkPolicy is applied by the CNI, there is a brief window with open access. Use init containers or startup probes to delay agent start until policy is confirmed active.

If an allowed service changes its IP or hostname and the allowlist is not updated, the agent's legitimate operations fail. Monitor for DNS resolution failures and broken connections to allowed endpoints as a service health signal.

If the network policy engine fails, define whether the fallback is fail-open (all traffic allowed) or fail-closed (all traffic blocked). Always choose fail-closed for security-critical agent sandboxes, even if it means brief service disruption.

Network isolation enforces a default-deny posture at the kernel or CNI layer, ensuring that even a fully compromised agent cannot reach unauthorized network destinations regardless of what its code or model output says.

The correct model is an explicit allowlist of approved destinations -- not a blocklist of known-bad ones. All unlisted destinations are denied by default; all unlisted ports and protocols are denied even for approved hosts.
DNS control is the first and most robust layer: an agent that cannot resolve a domain name cannot reach it, regardless of what IP address it might be at.

Network isolation operates below the application layer that prompt injection controls -- it blocks exfiltration at the packet level, independent of what the LLM outputs and independent of any application-level guardrails.
All blocked connection attempts must be logged and shipped to an external SIEM. Blocked connections are the primary signal for detecting active exfiltration attempts.

Combine iptables/NetworkPolicy (L3/L4), DNS sinkholing, and a TLS-inspecting forward proxy for comprehensive coverage. No single layer is sufficient against a sophisticated attacker using covert channels.

The total latency overhead of all network isolation controls combined is typically under 25ms per agent interaction -- less than 5% of inference latency -- making this a high-security/low-cost control for production deployments.
